note:
根据我自己的测试和使用情况，并且在相当一段时间内（除了一些极端的问题）都没有发现严重的错误，看来NovelWriter对于1.0版本来说足够稳定。感谢所有不断提供有关错误，外观问题或提出改进建议和新功能的反馈的新用户。我很高兴听到其他人发现我的应用程序很有用，随着我自己得到新的建议和新的想法，我将不断进行改进。同时，我将继续保持NovelWriter的简洁和干净，并避免功能过大。
 此版本主要解决了用户界面上的修饰和其他次要问题，并对一些较少使用的功能进行了一些小的改进。除此之外，自上次发布候选版本以来，没有什么重大变化。
 此版本结束了对该项目的两年修改。该项目源于我以前的工作与同事玛丽安·吕克霍夫（MarianLückhof）进行的多次午餐和咖啡讨论。我们俩都在寻找一种适合我们需求的在Linux上写小说的工具。我们开始组装已成为NovelWriter的功能的愿望清单。此外，GitHub上的用户继续测试新功能，提供非常有用的反馈，并提出改进建议。尤其是@johnblommers的反馈在最初的大部分开发过程中都很有帮助。在过去的几个月中，更多的用户开始发表想法和反馈。感谢大家的贡献。
 1.0版本旨在作为NovelWriter核心功能的第一个版本。这并不意味着所有计划中的功能都已完全实现。有很多想法和建议可供考虑和实施。欢迎提出新的想法和建议。可以在[讨论页面]（https://github.com/vkbo/novelWriter/discussions）上讨论是否作为问题跟踪器中的功能请求，或者是否完整构建。
   修复了＆＃34; Distraction Free模式＆＃34;旁边的复选框的一个小修饰问题。菜单中的选中标记始终不对应于该模式的当前状态的菜单项。 PR＃532。
 打开“写作统计”时在新项目中没有会话日志文件的对话框中，将弹出一个错误对话框，提示该文件丢失。丢失的文件不是错误，应该安静地忽略它。 PR＃535。
 不要在写入文档开头的元数据行中强制使用字符串数据类型。某些条目可能具有NoneType，然后强制类型将导致崩溃。 PR＃539。 
修复了索引和轮廓详细信息面板中的一些错误检查。用户操作无法进行检查，但是检查到位可以捕获编码错误。 PR＃549。
  ＆＃34; Build Novel Project＆＃34;中的占位符文本该工具之前使用的标签是指构建按钮的名称。现在，它引用了当前按钮上的标签。 PR＃535。
 添加＆＃34;向上移动项目＆＃34;和＆＃34;将项目下移＆＃34;到项目树上下文菜单。它们连接到与“工具”菜单中相同条目相同的功能。 PR＃535。
  特殊的“孤立的文件”文件夹已被删除。由于文档类将大多数文档元数据保存到文档文件的标题中，因此不再严格必须，并且由于孤立的文件夹不是跟踪文件夹，因此它确实使项目树后面的代码复杂化，因此需要一个合理的选择。少量定制代码以适合其余树数据模型。在项目存储文件夹中找到的项目文件中不存在的文件现在将基于一组后备时间被导入到主项目树中。所有恢复的文件均以单词＆＃34; Recovered＆＃34;为前缀。问题＃540，PR＃543。
 在不遵循严格的标题层级逻辑的情况下，更改了将新颖的标题添加到“大纲”视图的方式。先前。不在下一章的场景标题将添加到上一章。这可能有点令人困惑。现在，相反，章节外的场景将被提升一个级别。 PR＃549。
  修复了贡献准则中的一些小错别字或错误的单词错误。 PR＃537，作者Curtis Gedak @gedakc。
  更新了文档，进行了最新更改，并重写了一些部分以使术语更加一致。 PR＃548。 
更新了打开问题消息框的代码的各个部分，并将调用重定向到主GUI类。 这样做主要是为了保持一致性，但是较早才添加了此功能，以确保核心类不依赖于Qt库。 PR＃535。
note_en:
Based on my own testing and usage, and no serious bugs discovered in quite some time (aside from a few corner case issues), it appears that novelWriter is stable enough for a 1.0 release. Thanks to all the new users who keep providing feedback on bugs, cosmetic issues, or suggesting improvements and new features. I'm glad to hear that others find my application useful, and I will keep making improvements as I get new suggestions and have new ideas myself. At the same time, I will continue to keep novelWriter simple and clean and avoid feature-bloat.
 This release mainly fixes cosmetic and other minor issues to the user interface and makes a few minor improvements to some less used features. Aside from this, nothing major has changed since the last release candidate.
 This release concludes over two years of tinkering with this project. The project grew out of numerous lunch and coffee discussions with my colleague Marian Lückhof at my former job. We were both looking for a tool for writing novels on Linux that suited our needs. We started assembling a wish list of features that has become novelWriter. In addition, users on GitHub have continued to test new features, provide very helpful feedback, and make new suggestions for improvements. Especially the feedback from  @johnblommers has been helpful during much of the initial development time. Over the last months more users have started posting ideas and feedback. Thanks to all of you for your contributions.
 The 1.0 release is intended as a first release of the core features of novelWriter. That does not mean that all planned features have been fully implemented. There is a long list of ideas and suggestions to consider and implement. New ideas and suggestions are welcome. Either as feature requests in the issue tracker, or if not fully formed, can be discussed on the [discussions page] ( https://github.com/vkbo/novelWriter/discussions).
   Fixed a minor cosmetic issue with the checkbox next to the "Distraction Free Mode" entry in the menu where its checkmark wouldn't always correspond to the current state of the mode. PR  #532.
 When opening the "Writing Stats" dialog in a new project where there is no session log file yet, an error dialog would pop up to complain the file is missing. A missing file is not an error, and should just be quietly ignored. PR  #535.
 Don't enforce string data type in meta data lines written to the head of documents. Some of the entries can potentially be of NoneType, and the enforced type will then cause a crash. PR  #539.
 Fixed a couple of faulty checks in the index and outline details panel. The checks were not reachable by user actions, but put in place to capture coding errors. PR  #549.
  The placeholder text in the "Build Novel Project" tool was referring to the name of the build button by a previous label. It now refers to the label that is on the current button. PR  #535.
 Add "Move Item Up" and "Move Item Down" to the project tree context menu. These connect to the same function as the same entries in the Tools menu. PR  #535.
  The special "Orphaned Files" folder has been dropped. Since the document class saves most of the document meta data to the header of document files, it is no longer strictly necessary and it does complicate the code behind the project tree as the orphaned folder isn't a tracked folder and therefore needs a fair bit of customised code to fit into the rest of the tree data model. Files found in the project's storage folder that do not exist in the project file will now be imported into the main project tree based on a set of fallbacks. All recovered files are prefixed with the word "Recovered". Issue  #540, PR  #543.
 Changed the way novel headers are added to the Outline view in cases where the strict logic of header levelled isn't obeyed. Previously. a scene header not under a chapter would be added to a previous chapter. That may be a bit confusing. Now, instead, a scene outside a chapter will just be bumped up one level. PR  #549.
  Fixed some minor typo or wrong word errors in the contributing guidelines. PR  #537 by Curtis Gedak  @gedakc.
  Updated documentation with latest changes and rewritten some sections to make the terminology more consistent. PR  #548.
  Updated various parts of the code where a question message box is opened and redirected the call to the main GUI class. This was done mostly for consistency, but the feature was added earlier to ensure that core classes do not depend on Qt libraries. PR  #535.
title: novelWriter 1.0

note:
去年12月筹集的1.32亿美元资金将帮助该公司扩大规模并支付升级费用，包括与大多数电子病历的集成-预计第二季度将实现这一集成。
 整个2020年，K Health充分利用了其在机器学习和消费者医疗保健交叉口的经营地位，一年内筹集了2.22亿美元。
  投资者的这种胃口表明，随着公司希望使用技术使人们负担得起的医疗服务，消费者医疗保健领域将有很大的机会。
 对于K Health而言，这意味着每月订购9美元的服务即可无限制地访问平台上的服务和医生，以及每月19美元的虚拟心理健康服务和19美元的一次性紧急护理咨询费用。
 对于患者和投资者来说，K Health已成功通过与以色列健康维护组织Maccabi Healthcare Services等组织合作获取了数据，该组织放弃了数十年的患者和健康结果匿名数据，以训练K Health的预测算法，评估患者并协助公司医生进行诊断。
 从理论上讲，这意味着该公司的服务本质上是充当虚拟的初级保健医生，持有大量的患者信息，这些信息一起使用时，可能能够更快地发现潜在的医疗状况或提供对患者护理的更全面的了解。
 对于制药公司而言，这可能意味着深入了解人群健康状况，这可能是药物开发的潜在获利途径。 
据该平台上的一位提供者称，该公司的心理健康服务使用未经许可的精神科医生来进行评估和评估，这可能导致与未经培训的医生进行交流，造成的弊大于利。
 尽管公司首席执行官Allon Bloch认为大多数服务可以远程执行（Bloch估计这一数字为90％）是正确的，但应由经过必要培训的专业人员进行远程执行。
 在医疗保健方面，算法或通才应该承担多少繁重工作是有限制的，而且看来K Health希望突破这些限制。
 布洛赫说：“药物转诊，急性问题，预防问题，其中大多数可以远程完成。” “有机会做得更好，甚至可能更便宜。
 Bloch表示，K Health已经通过其急诊服务或订阅服务接待了成千上万的患者，并在2020年创造了数千万的收入。他拒绝透露有多少患者使用了紧急护理服务，而不是每月订购的服务。
 与其他远程提供服务的公司一样，远程医疗公司在大流行期间蓬勃发展。虚拟医学的两个早期先驱Teladoc和Amwell的股价飞涨。像Hims这样的公司会提供选择条件的处方，而这些条件不一定是健康的，特殊目的的收购公司所需要的，其估值为16亿美元。
 支持K Health的是由GGV Capital和Valor Equity Partners领导的一群投资者。 Kaiser Permanente的退休基金和3G Capital（拥有Burger King和Kraft Heinz的巴西投资公司）的所有者的投资办公室以及14W，Max Ventures，Pico Partners，Marcy Venture Partners，Primary Venture Partners和BoxGroup也参加了会议在全面。 
与该公司合作的组织包括Maccabi Healthcare； Mayo诊所，正在与该公司一起研究虚拟护理模型； 还有Anthem，上面用白色标记了K Health服务，并将其提供给保险公司数百万名会员中的一些人。
note_en:
The $132 million round raised in December will help the company expand and help pay for upgrades including an integration with most electronic health records — an integration that’s expected by the second quarter.
 Throughout 2020 K Health has leveraged its position operating at the intersection of machine learning and consumer healthcare to raised $222 million in a single year.
  This appetite from investors shows how large the opportunity is in consumer healthcare as companies look to use technology to make care more affordable.
 For K Health, that means a monthly subscription to its service of $9 for unlimited access to the service and physicians on the platform, as well as a $19 per-month virtual mental health offering and a $19 fee for a one-time urgent care consultation.
 To patients and investors the pitch is that the data K Health has managed to acquire through partnerships with organizations like the Israel health maintenance organization  Maccabi Healthcare Services, which gave up decades of anonymized data on patients and health outcomes to train K Health’s predictive algorithm, can assess patients and aid the in diagnoses for the company’s doctors.
 In theory that means the company’s service essentially acts as a virtual primary care physician, holding a wealth of patient information that, when taken together, might be able to spot underlying medical conditions faster or provide a more holistic view into patient care.
 For pharmaceutical companies that could mean insights into population health that could be potentially profitable avenues for drug discovery.
  The company’s mental health offering uses medical doctors who are not licensed psychiatrists to perform their evaluations and assessments, according to one provider on the platform, which can lead to interactions with untrained physicians that can cause more harm than good.
 While company chief executive Allon Bloch is likely correct in his assessment that most services can be performed remotely (Bloch puts the figure at 90%), they should be performed remotely by professionals who have the necessary training.
 There are limits to how much heavy lifting an algorithm or a generalist should do when it comes to healthcare, and it appears that K Health wants to push those limits.
 “Drug referrals, acute issues, prevention issues, most of those can be done remotely,” Bloch said. “There’s an opportunity to do much better and potentially cheaper.
 K Health has already seen hundreds of thousands of patients either through its urgent care offering or its subscription service and generated tens of millions in revenue in 2020, according to Bloch. He declined to disclose how many patients used the urgent care service vs. the monthly subscription offering.
 Telemedicine companies, like other companies providing services remotely, have thrived during the pandemic. Teladoc and Amwell, two of the early pioneers in virtual medicine have seen their share prices soar. Companies like Hims, that provide prescriptions for elective conditions that aren’t necessarily covered by health, special purpose acquisition companies  at valuations of $1.6 billion.
 Backing K Health are a group of investors led by GGV Capital and Valor Equity Partners. Kaiser Permanente’s pension fund and the investment offices of the owners of 3G Capital (the Brazilian investment firm that owns Burger King and Kraft Heinz), along with  14W, Max Ventures, Pico Partners, Marcy Venture Partners, Primary Venture Partners and BoxGroup, also participated in the round.
 Organizations working with the company include Maccabi Healthcare; the Mayo Clinic, which is investigating virtual care models with the company; and Anthem, which has white labeled the K Health service and provides it to some of the insurer’s millions of members.
title: K Health扩展到虚拟育儿领域，以15亿美元的估值筹集了1.32亿美元
title_en: K Health expands into virtual childcare and raises $132 million at a $1.5 billion valuation

note:
思杰已经确认了本周早些时候的报道，它以22.5亿美元的全现金交易收购了以企业为中心的工作管理平台Wrike。
 总部位于圣何塞的Wrike成立于2006年，是一个项目管理平台，通过软件即服务（SaaS）模型出售给企业，从而使团队可以监视和跟踪项目，工作流，截止日期等。该公司声称包括Google，戴尔，Snowflake，Okta和Airbnb在内的一些著名客户，已经筹集了约2600万美元的外部资金，但在2018年被Vista Equity Partners收购，但未披露金额。
  Citrix是一家总部位于加利福尼亚州圣克拉拉的30年前公开上市的软件公司，它提供了涵盖云计算，服务器，网络等更多工具。其中包括Citrix Workspace，这是一个虚拟化平台，可让企业远程部署应用程序和桌面。它使他们能够从任何地方监督和保护所有连接到其网络的设备，包括控制对应用程序和文件的访问，这似乎是Wrike将会居住的世界。
  这笔交易非常有意义，因为这两个平台在构建时都考虑了分散的劳动力。
 Citrix现在将能够为其包括Deloitte，Hewlett Packard和SAP在内的数千个客户提供基于云的协作工作管理工具。在远程工作已成为数百万人的普遍习惯的时候，这是很重要的。思杰表示，Wrike立即获得了成千上万客户的访问权，合并后的公司为40万客户提供服务。
 在Wrike领域运营的其他公司还包括Asana，该公司在几个月前以高涨的股票上市，目前市值超过60亿美元。
 思杰表示，两家公司将在交易完成之前独立运作，并有望在2021年上半年完成。Wrike创始人兼首席执行官安德鲁·Filev将在收购后继续领导Wrike平台，并将直接向Citrix CFO Arlen汇报。申克曼

note_en:
Citrix has  confirmed reports from earlier this week that it is buying enterprise-focused  work management platform Wrike in an all-cash deal worth $2.25 billion.
 Founded in 2006, San Jose-based  Wrike is a project management platform sold to businesses through a software-as-a-service (SaaS) model, allowing teams to monitor and track projects, workflows, deadlines, and more. The company claims some notable customers, including Google, Dell, Snowflake, Okta, and Airbnb, and had raised around $26 million in external funding, but it was  acquired by Vista Equity Partners in 2018 for an undisclosed amount.
  Citrix, a 30-year-old publicly traded software company based in Santa Clara, California, provides myriad tools spanning cloud computing, servers, networking, and more. Among these is  Citrix Workspace, a virtualization platform that allows enterprises to deploy apps and desktops remotely. It enables them to oversee and secure all the devices that connect to its network from wherever they are, including controlling access to apps and files, and this appears to be the world Wrike will inhabit.
  The deal makes a great deal of sense, as both platforms are essentially built with the distributed workforce in mind.
 Citrix will now be able to offer cloud-based collaborative work management tools to its thousands of customers, which include Deloitte, Hewlett Packard, and SAP. This is pertinent at a time when  remote work has become the norm for millions of people. And Wrike gains instant access to thousands more customers, with the combined company serving more than 400,000 clients, according to Citrix.
 Other players operating in Wrike’s space  include Asana, which  went public just a few months ago with soaring shares giving it a current market cap of more than $6 billion.
 Citrix said the two companies will operate independently until the deal closes, which it expects to happen in the first half of 2021. Wrike founder and CEO Andrew Filev will continue to head up the Wrike platform post-acquisition and will report directly to Citrix CFO Arlen Shenkman.

note:
总部位于纽约的K Health今天完成了由GGV Capital和Valor Equity Partners牵头的1.32亿美元E轮融资，使该公司迄今为止的融资总额超过2.71亿美元。伴随这一回合的结束，K Health推出了K for Parents，该计划为父母提供了与儿科医生的联系，以远程诊断和治疗3至17岁的儿童。
 随着大流行达到可怕的新高峰，对对话型机器人等分类技术的需求已急剧上升。根据美国疾病控制和预防中心（CDC）发布的一项研究，数百万患者至少需要两个小时才能去看保健提供者。像IBM，Facebook和Microsoft这样的科技巨头已经与政府和私营企业合作，推出了基于聊天机器人的解决方案，以应对这种情况。诸如Current Health和Twistle之类的公司已与Providence和其他医疗保健提供商合作，以试用家庭健康跟踪平台。甚至在大流行之前，十分之九的老年人表示，他们希望在未来十年内留在家中，这突显了对远程健康监控解决方案的需求。
  Wix和Vroom的前首席执行官联合创始人Allon Bloch认为，远程医疗可以帮助减少在美国定期就诊的昂贵费用。KHealth平台吸收了病史，临床结果以及超过10,000名医生的经验，可以在数百种疾病。
 近年来，得益于与Anthem等保险提供商的合作，K Health的业务得到了大幅扩展。该公司最近以西班牙语发布了其远程医疗服务，并与路易斯安那州立大学合作，在四月份为路易斯安那州居民提供免费的医生服务。
 新兴的远程医疗市场竞争激烈，据一些分析师估计，到2022年，该市场的价值将达到296亿美元。按需医生，HealthTap，PlushCare，Teladoc和American Well都在争夺一部分份额，仅举几例。但是Bloch断言，K Health的AI驱动方法与其专有的医学知识数据库相结合，使其具有优势。
 K Health的智能手机应用程序K（Bloch声称拥有超过400万成员，并且每天以10,000至15,000新用户的速度增长），促进了医生名册的应用程序内访问。K用户可以付费咨询，前提是他们居住在可提供服务的49个州之一。医生检查AI辅助的患者症状分解，然后根据需要诊断，开处方或转诊患者。
  K Health用户可以每月9美元，一次19美元的费用或每月29美元的三个月无限制访问的费用与医生聊天。还提供心理健康服务，处方药可以每月34美元的价格交付给用户。 
K来自20年数据库，其中包含以色列第二大卫生基金Maccabi提供的数以百万计的电子健康记录（包括医师记录和化验结果）以及数十亿次“健康事件”（包括恶心，头痛和呕吐），其中包括以及美国和墨西哥的卫生系统。用户首先下载适用于iOS或Android的应用，然后回答有关其年龄，性别，体重指数，健康史和症状的大约20个问题。机器学习支持的后端使用响应来构建私有概要文件，并将其与从超过4亿份临床笔记和图表中收集的见解进行比较。
 结果页面显示了在类似健康状况下人们所经历的结果列表，以及指示每次诊断可能性的百分比。如果用户愿意，可以在进行远程医疗或亲自预约之前通过符合HIPAA的消息传递功能与临床医生共享报告和个人资料。
 布洛赫（Bloch）指出，每年有26％的儿童前往急诊诊所就诊。他说，“家长陪伴”将为儿科医生提供远程访问，并允许家长与AI聊天以显示如何诊断出喜欢他们的孩子。 Bloch表示，K for Parents的设计是从数百名家长的反馈中得出的，“对教学动画的风格进行了认真的关注。”
 K Health最近与Anthem合作开发了K的联合品牌版本CareSpree，使Anthem的超过4000万成员与医生聊天的费用“不到共付额”。对于亲自就医，磁共振成像扫描和X射线，CareSpree使用户能够安排参与的医疗保健提供者的预约并支付预先协商的价格。
 另外，去年下半年，K Health宣布将与Mayo Clinic合作，整合医疗系统的Clinic Data Analytics Platform，该平台包括针对Mayo Clinic患者和医生的临床决策支持工具。
 K Health的部分资金来自总部位于以色列特拉维夫的健康维护组织（HMO）Maccabi Health及其技术孵化机构莫里斯·卡恩研究与创新研究所。该公司表示，从该应用程序获得的利润被重新投资到建立K Health的数据库中。最新一期的其他投资者包括LTS Ventures，Atreides Ventures，14W，Max Ventures，Pico Partners，Marcy Venture Partners，Primary Ventures和Box Group。

New York City-based  K Health today closed a $132 million series E funding round led by GGV Capital and Valor Equity Partners, bringing the company’s total raised to date to over $271 million. In conjunction with the round’s closure, K Health launched K for Parents, a program offering parents access to a pediatrician to remotely diagnose and treat children ages 3 to 17.
 The demand for triaging technologies like conversational bots has risen sharply as the pandemic reaches frightening new peaks. Millions of patients wait at least two hours to see a health care provider,  according to a study published by the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Tech giants like IBM, Facebook, and Microsoft have partnered with governments and private industry to roll out chatbot-based solutions in response, as have a  number of startups. Companies like  Current Health and Twistle have teamed up with Providence and other health care providers to pilot at-home health-tracking platforms. Even before the pandemic, nine in 10 seniors  said they’d prefer to stay in their homes over the next 10 years, highlighting the need for remote health monitoring solutions.
  Cofounder Allon Bloch, previously CEO of Wix and Vroom, believes telemedicine could help reduce the often exorbitant costs of regular doctor’s visits in the U.S. K Health’s platform ingests medical histories, clinical outcomes, and the experience of more than 10,000 doctors to deliver treatment information on hundreds of diseases.
 In recent years, K Health has expanded substantially thanks to partnerships with insurance providers like Anthem. The company recently released its telemedical services in Spanish and worked with Louisiana State University to extend free access to doctors for Louisiana residents during the month of April.
 There’s fierce competition in the budding telemedicine market, which some analysts estimate could be worth $29.6 billion by 2022. Doctor on Demand, HealthTap, PlushCare, Teladoc, and American Well are all competing for a slice of the pie, to name just a few. But Bloch asserts that K Health’s AI-driven approach, combined with its proprietary medical knowledge database, gives it a leg up.
 K Health’s smartphone app, K — which Bloch claims has more than 4 million members, growing at a rate of 10,000 to 15,000 new users a day — facilitates in-app visits from a roster of doctors K users can consult for a fee, assuming they live in one of the 49 states where service is available. The doctors review an AI-assisted breakdown of the patient’s symptoms and then diagnose, prescribe, or refer the patient as appropriate.
  K Health users can chat with a doctor for $9 per month, $19 for a one-time session, or $29 for three-month unlimited access. Mental health services are also offered, and prescribed medication can be delivered to users for $34 per month.
 K sources from a 20-year database of millions of electronic health records (including physician notes and lab results) and billions of “health events” — including nausea, headaches, and vomiting — supplied by Maccabi, Israel’s second-largest health fund, as well as health systems in the U.S. and Mexico. Users start by downloading an app for iOS or Android and answering roughly 20 questions about their age, gender, body mass index, health history, and symptoms. A machine learning-powered backend uses the responses to build a private profile, which it compares to insights gleaned from a corpus of over 400 million clinical notes and charts.
 The results page shows a list of outcomes experienced by people in similar health circumstances, along with a percentage indicating the likelihood of each diagnosis. Reports and profiles can be shared with clinicians via a HIPAA-compliant messaging feature ahead of telemedical or in-person appointments, if users so choose.
 Bloch, who notes that 26% of children visit urgent care clinics annually, says that K for Parents will provide remote access to pediatricians and allow parents to chat with an AI to show how children like theirs were diagnosed. K for Parents was designed with feedback from hundreds of parents, according to Bloch, with “thoughtful attention to the style of instructional animations.”
 K Health recently partnered with Anthem to develop a cobranded version of K, CareSpree, that lets Anthem’s over 40 million members chat with a doctor for “less than a copay.” For in-person doctor visits, magnetic resonance imaging scans, and X-rays, CareSpree enables users to schedule appointments at participating health care providers and pay a prenegotiated price.
 Separately, K Health late last year announced it’ll collaborate with the Mayo Clinic to integrate the health system’s Clinic Data Analytics Platform, which includes clinical decision support tools for Mayo Clinic patients and doctors.
 K Health is funded in part by Tel Aviv, Israel-based health maintenance organization (HMO) Maccabi Health and its tech incubation arm, Morris Kahn Institute for Research and Innovation. Profits from the app are reinvested into building K Health’s database, the company says. Additional investors in the latest tranche include LTS Ventures, Atreides Ventures, 14W, Max Ventures, Pico Partners, Marcy Venture Partners, Primary Ventures, and Box Group.

This is the Computer History Wiki ('CHWiki', for short), a knowledge base about historic computers which anyone can join in order to edit!
 (Due to past problems with spam, anonymous editing and automatic account creation have had to be disabled; you'll have to contact an admin to get an account - see below.)
    This wiki is an experiment to create a means for knowledgeable people to enter their information into some kind of a knowledge base. This is however, not the relatively formalized tone and style imposed by Wikipedia. Sentences starting with "I seem to recall" are perfectly welcome here - and articles do  not have to be in the descriptive, encyclopedic style Wikipedia enforces. On the contrary, it is  preferred that many of the articles be references, guides, helpful hints, suggestions,  tutorials and so on.
 If you have any information you want to contribute to this wiki,  please, contribute it. We'd much rather remove or modify a dozen potentially unsuited submissions than miss out on a single good one. We hope this wiki grows into a useful resource for everyone interested in classic computing, no matter what their level of knowledge may be; it is meant to be all-encompassing. --  Tore &  Lucky 16:11, 16 May 2007 (PDT)
  As noted above, due to past problems with spam, you need an account to edit, and admins have to manually create accounts.  Tore, who handles requests for accounts filed via the system, is often busy, but I too am happy to create accounts - just email me (jnc at alum dot mit dot edu). Please let me know what account name you'd like, and what email address to associate with it, and I'll try and get right to it. The system will send you an email with a temporary password; if you don't see it, and I have sent you an acknowledgement, check your spam-traps: we've had issues with that.  Jnc ( talk) 15:33, 22 October 2017 (CEST)
  If you're not sure what to add, the  Wanted pages page is always a good place to start! Right at the moment, two  Apple II pages are top of the list. So if you have any expertise there, please weigh in!  Jnc ( talk) 21:44, 2 August 2017 (CEST)
 The other thing you could do is fill out a stub article. (These are articles that need improving, by having more information added to them. They're a perfectly good start for a great article, they just haven't grown up yet.) You can find a list of them  here.  Jnc ( talk) 23:10, 17 December 2018 (CET)
  Tore has added me to the  admin group, and I have considerable experience as an admin on  Wikipedia, so if anyone needs anything which requires admin status (e.g. merging histories for duplicate articles, deleting article names with typos, etc, etc) please let me know. 在最新的发展中，伦敦的一家初创公司PPRO建立了一个平台，使市场，支付提供商和其他电子商务参与者更容易实现本地化付款，即以当地客户喜欢的任何形式进行付款使用范围远远超出基本的支付卡-已完成一轮1.8亿美元的融资，这使PPRO的估值超过了10亿美元。
 PPRO（在付款专业人士中被称为“ P-pro”）计划利用这笔资金继续在新市场中扩张。
  PPRO的首席执行官西蒙·布莱克（Simon Black）在接受采访时表示，来年的两个特别关注的领域将是在新加坡和印尼等亚洲国家以及拉丁美洲（该公司在当地收购了本地玩家allpago）的更多活动。 2019。
 在这两种情况下，机遇都来自于更多的在线交易，以及零散的支付市场的混乱带来的高增长。
 资金来自包括Eurazeo Growth，Sprints Capital和Wellington Management在内的一组投资者。在去年八月该公司从Sprints以及Citi和HPE Growth筹集了5000万美元之后，它又获得了一笔融资。并在战略投资者PayPal的领导下于2018年又筹集了5,000万美元。
 贝宝（PalPal）与花旗银行（Citi），万事达卡（Mastercard）支付网关服务，莫利（Mollie）和世界支付（Worldpay）都是PPRO的100个大型全球客户之一，这些客户使用该公司的API来实现多种功能，包括本地化网关，处理和商户收单服务。
 消费者和企业在网上购买更多商品的活动泛滥（大流行的副产品，导致许多企业暂时关闭实体运营），这使该公司在2020年第四季度到2019年同一季度之间的交易量翻了一番。 
金融服务总体上是分散的-实际上，只有少数几种交易可以进行（通常是：存款，付款，信贷），但实际上是成千上万种针对特定市场及其人口的排列和方法。通常会结合他们自己的本地化选择。
 这导致了许多提供“银行即服务”或“金融科技即服务”的公司的兴起，其中，技术提供商在后台将多种服务（有时是数千种）结合在一起，借助API，客户可以更轻松地将这些服务插入自己的客户，以便更轻松地使用，这些服务通常与提供给他们的一系列其他服务（如资金管理）相关。
 包括付款和其他金融科技服务在内的这一更广阔领域的其他公司包括Rapyd，Mambu，Thought Machine，Temenos，Edera，Adyen，Stripe和诸如Unit之类的新兴公司，其中许多公司最近尤其在筹集大量资金将目前快速发展的市场扩大一倍。
 PPRO的独特之处在于，它是为在多个国家或地区运营的公司识别付款分散难题方面的先行者，并且它仅继续在付款中发挥作用，而没有跳到邻近服务。
 布莱克说：“我们非常专注，因为本地支付问题实际上正在加剧，”布莱克认为，“消费者想要使用的东西，他们的胃口与支付方式的泛滥之间的脱节”都加剧了复杂性（权衡是消费者的更多选择，但同样可能是太多的选择？）。
 正如布莱克所看到的那样，该公司对付款的关注使它有更多的动力来建设更好的技术，专门用于在全球范围内解决这一问题。
 “ PPRO正在为提高工业实力建立解决方案。它之所以迅速成长是因为没有其他公司真正具有全球影响力。我们正在全球化，以支持想要国有化的客户的需求，因此我们有机会专注于支付，成为战略外包合作伙伴。” 
这并不意味着没有扩展产品的空间：除了支付，Black还强调了产品合规性，并提供了更好的分析，这是公司已经活跃并且将为客户做更多事情的两个领域。
 他指出：“我们合作并提供价值的地方在于预期消费者需求的变化。” “我们监视客户如何使用这些方法，并且-无论您是服务提供商，家具还是旅游公司-都确定哪种是最相关的付款方式。”他说，诸如开放银行，银行允许直接从客户帐户进行付款的工具或“立即购买以后付款”等服务，就是有更多机会的领域的例子。
 Eurazeo Growth董事总经理NathalieKornhoff-Brüls在一份声明中说：“我们很高兴为Simon和PPRO团队提供支持，因为他们将继续开发一流的本地支付解决方案。” “未来的所有迹象都表明，在付款方式的创新仍然强劲的情况下，数字商务，甚至跨境商务将继续呈指数级增长。结果，便利本地支付变得越来越复杂。但是，付款服务提供商已不再具有选择权，因为商家及其客户都在推动采用。”
 “ PPRO被证明是该领域的首选解决方案，它提供了全球最大的支付公司所依赖的本地支付技术和专业知识。 Sprints Capital的合伙人Voria Fattahi补充说：“我们的投资反映了我们对PPRO增长潜力的信心，我们很高兴为PPRO及其团队提供支持。
note_en:
In the latest development,  PPRO, a London-based startup that has built a platform to make it easier for marketplaces, payment providers and other e-commerce players to enable localised payments — that is, make and take payments in whatever form local customers prefer to use, which extend well beyond basic payment cards — has closed a round of $180 million, funding that catapults PPRO’s valuation to over $1 billion.
 PPRO (pronounced “P-pro”, as in payments professionals) plans to use the funding to continue expanding in newer markets.
  Simon Black, PPRO’s CEO, said in an interview that two particular areas of focus in the coming year will be more activity in Asian countries like Singapore and Indonesia, as well as Latin America, where the company acquired a local player,  allpago, back in 2019.
 In both cases, the opportunity comes in the form of high growth stemming from more transactions moving online, as well as the chaos that is the fragmented payments market.
 The capital is coming from a group of investors that includes Eurazeo Growth, Sprints Capital, and Wellington Management. It comes on the heels of a $50 million round the company  raised last August from Sprints, along with Citi and HPE Growth; and a further  $50 million it picked up in 2018 led by strategic investor PayPal.
 PayPal, alongside Citi, Mastercard Payment Gateway Services, Mollie, and Worldpay are among PPRO’s 100 large global customers, which use the company’s APIs for a variety of functions, including localised gateway, processing and merchant acquirer services.
 The flood of activity coming from consumers and businesses buying more online — a by-product of the pandemic leading to many businesses shutting down physical operations for the moment — has seen the company double transaction volumes between Q4 2020 and the same quarter in 2019.
  The fragmentation of financial services overall — where realistically, there is only handful of types of transactions that might be made (usually: deposits, payments, credit), but quite literally thousands of permutations and methods to make them, with specific markets and their populations typically coalescing around their own localised selections.
 That has led to the rise of a number of companies providing what has come to be called “banking as a service” or “fintech as a service,” where a tech provider stitches together in the background a number of services, sometimes thousands, and makes it easier for their customers, by way of an API, to plug those services in for their own customers to use more easily, most often connected to a range of other services provided to them like money management.
 Others in this wider space that includes payments and other fintech services include the likes of  Rapyd,  Mambu,  Thought Machine,  Temenos,  Edera, Adyen, Stripe and newer players like  Unit, with many of these raising large amounts of money in recent times in particular to double down on what is currently a rapidly expanding market.
 The unique aspect of PPRO is that it was an early mover in the area of identifying the conundrum of fragmentation in payments for companies that operate in more than one country or region, and that it has continued to play only in payments, without a jump to adjacent services.
 “We’re ultra focused because the local payments problem is actually growing,” said Black, who believes that “the disconnect between what a consumer wants to use, but also their appetite and the proliferation of payment options” all contribute to more complexity (with the trade-off being more choices for consumers, but equally possibly too much choice?).
 As Black sees it, the company’s focus on payments has given it more momentum to build better tech specifically to address that globally.
 “PPRO is building solutions for performance in industrial strength. It’s growing rapidly because there are no other players that are truly global. We are globalizing to support the needs of customers who want to nationalize, so we have an opportunity to focus on payments, to be a strategic outsource partner.”
 This doesn’t mean that there isn’t room for product expansion: alongside payments, Black highlighted product compliance and providing better analytics as two areas where the company is already active and will be doing more for customers.
 “Where we partner and provide value is in anticipating changes in consumer demand,” he noted. “We monitor how customers are using those methods and — whether you are are service provider or furniture or travel company — determine which are the best relevant payment methods.” Services like open banking, tools for banks to enable allowing payments directly from customers’ accounts, or buy-now-pay-later payments, are examples, he said, of areas that speak of further opportunities.
 “We are delighted to support Simon and the team at PPRO as they continue to develop best-in-class local payment solutions,” commented Nathalie Kornhoff-Brüls, Managing Director at Eurazeo Growth, in a statement. “All signs for the future indicate that digital commerce, and even more so cross-border commerce, will continue to grow exponentially while innovation in payment methods remains strong. As a result, facilitating local payments is becoming increasingly complex. Payment service providers, however, no longer have a choice as merchants and their customers are pushing for the adoption.”
 “PPRO has proven to be the go-to problem solver in this area, providing the local payments technology and expertise that the world’s biggest payment players rely on. Our investment reflects our confidence in the growth potential for PPRO and we’re excited to support PPRO and its team on their journey,” added Voria Fattahi, a partner at Sprints Capital, in a separate statement.
title: PPRO以$ 1B +的估值获得1.8亿美元的融资，将零散的支付世界融合在一起
title_en: PPRO nabs $180M at a $1B+ valuation to bring together the fragmented world of payments

note_en: Hyperlocal delivery startup  Dunzo has raised $40 million in Series E funding from new and existing investors including  Google, Lightbox, Evolvence, Hana Financial Investment, LGT Lightstone Aspada, and Alteria among others.  The company said it plans to focus on sustainable growth across its fastest-growing cities such as Mumbai, Chennai, and Pune in the coming year.  Dunzo said it witnessed strong organic user demand in the pandemic-hit 2020 and is now approximately a $100 million business in annualised gross merchandise value (GMV), a two-fold increase over the past year.  "As a team, we are more focused than ever to enable local merchants to get closer to their users and build one of the most loved consumer brands in the country," said Dunzo cofounder and chief executive Kabeer Biswas.  The Bengaluru-based startup said it has supported over 300 neighbourhoods across eight cities in the past six months, delivering essential goods to users within 29 minutes on average.  "As merchants go digital, Dunzo is helping small businesses in their digital transformation journey in support of business recovery," said Caesar Sengupta, vice-president, Google. "Through our India Digitization Fund, we’re committed to partnering with India’s innovative startups to build a truly inclusive digital economy that will benefit everyone."   This financing comes at a time when the hyper-local delivery sector is heating up with the recent entry of Flipkart and heightened focus from existing players like Amazon and Swiggy in the segment, with more consumers preferring doorstep deliveries due to the virus outbreak.

小时候，我的梦想工作是骑马。我不想成为一个骑马的女孩，而是一个骑马的女孩。那时对我来说，现在和现在都有意义。人们尊重马，马的声誉不可估量。他们的工作通常包括清晰，简洁的任务，例如将犁从田间的一端拉到另一端，或将孩子围成圈围着围成圈的圈。他们的基本需求也普遍得到满足：一天结束时，他们被喂饱了干草，他们的小铁鞋被清理干净，然后在高天花板的房子里睡觉。
 我的梦想没有实现，但是当我进入工作队伍时，我对梦想工作实际上的初见就加深了。我想做一些自己关心的事情，并在此过程中照顾自己的生活，但是我始终了解到，即使世界上最令人满意，薪水最高的工作也仍然可以工作。毕竟，没有人看到一匹马在拉着手推车，并认为自己没有劳动。在下班时间，马匹不会和顽强的9岁小孩子搭圈。没有人对一匹马说：“如果你热爱自己的工作，那你就永远不会有一天工作。”
 在一个稳定，全职工作正在消失的时代，其中某些事情才变得更加明显，包括我自己，坚持从事任何工作是其自身的壮举。经济政策研究所（Economic Policy Institute）计算得出，对大流行病的政治反应失败，已经使大约16％的劳动力（2680万人）失业或就业，但却失去了工作时间和薪水。而且工作并不一定能抵御困境，即使在当前的经济衰退之前，即使全职工作，九分之一的工人的工资也太低而无法摆脱贫困。但是梦dream以求的想法仍然令人沮丧。有很多自助文章，宣传如何在年底前找到理想工作的秘诀，或9个真正的步骤来退出工作并实现梦想。确实，Indeed.com列出了25个理想工作的信息，其中四分之三的国民平均年薪低于50,000美元。
  一项床垫公司实习，其中“鼓励”打Sn睡的人在工作期间睡觉，这实际上是一项理想的工作，收到了2,000多名申请人。 （一个Snoozetern告诉我，她实际上并没有最终睡在这份工作上，但是她发现实习计划仍然很令人满意。）Reddit上有一些通常浪漫的工作的线索—公园护林员，动物园管理员，飞行员—在海报上询问有关以下内容的详细问题如何在这些领域之一中真正谋生（“我爱动物，但也没有接受过比猫和狗更大的动物训练或特殊训练。在有大动物的动物园里，我能做些什么吗？ ？”）。
 一些梦dream以求的工作清单像病毒一样传播开来，例如旧金山东兄弟灯塔站的那张广告，上面刊登了一对夫妇的空缺，他们要在一家历史悠久的灯塔式旅馆里生活和工作，每年的总收入为13万美元。人们想像田园风光的生活在海洋上-至关重要的是，灯塔没有Wi-Fi-East Brother Light Station最终提出了数千个应用程序，其中只有60个来自真正合格的夫妇，因为这项工作需要有效的美国海岸警卫队执照。但是，差不多一年后，《旧金山纪事报》采访了担任该职位的幸运人士蒂芙尼·丹斯和泰勒·沃特森，梦想似乎更像是一份工作。 “我们是接待台。我们经营礼品店。我们整理床铺。我们做饭。泰勒是您的上尉和当地历史指南，”丹塞说。这对夫妻告诉《纪事报》，他们每周工作80至90小时。
  不难看出为什么这些工作如此诱人。梦job以求的概念仍然存在，可能是因为我们中的许多人都在已故的戴维·格雷伯（David Graeber）所谓的“废话工作”中工作，或者根本就没有工作。找到理想的工作是一个诱人的主意：FIRE运动的干练，新教版本–与其尝试逃脱工作，不如不去爱工作呢？
 这是一种与时俱进的冲动，但我想大多数梦想的工作更像是为40名有薪客人提供灯塔住宿和早餐，而不是带薪假期。安德鲁·史密斯（Andrew Smith）在冰川国家公园（Glacier National Park）担任过四个夏天的解释性游侠，他告诉我说他热爱自己的工作，主要是在公园游客和公园本身之间进行调解。成为公园护林员显然有好处-日复一日地了解自然景点是一种亲密的体验，游客无法体验。史密斯（Smith）居住在公园房屋中，很高兴自己的日子主要是在户外度过，这是许多地铁锁定的中层管理人员的梦想，即使其中很大一部分涉及与沮丧的游客打交道，他们在受欢迎的小路找不到停车位。 
但是史密斯告诉我，他仍然将自己的工作看作是工作，并且常常会遇到所有的危险。史密斯说：“做自己喜欢做的​​事不同于业余爱好。” “与穿休闲服一样，在公园里徒步旅行与从根本上来说是完全不同的体验。”由于史密斯（Smith）和大多数国家公园的雇员一样，都是季节性工人（现在他不在公园里工作），因此他的工作和福利虽然在公园季节很不错，但却不稳定。史密斯（Smith）告诉我，有些人在26岁时离开公园服务，无法再继续为其父母提供健康保险。在淡季，史密斯担任代课老师。除非他找到一份罕见的永久性公园工作，否则他就不会将其视为长期职业。
  菜谱食谱测试员Caroline Lange同样很幸运，她喜欢自己所做的事。兰格说：“我的朋友说这就像是一份rom-com的工作，我认为这确实很准确。” “直到遇到遇到它的人，您才意识到它的存在。”但是测试食谱并不像住在南希·迈耶斯（Nancy Meyers）的电影中一样-兰格说她的大部分工作实际上不是做饭，而是偷杂货。这对身体的要求比人们意识到的要高（她一天中大部分时间都在站着），并且由于演出是自由职业者，因此Lange没有带薪病假，并且可以享受最基本的国家兑换计划的保险，该计划具有巨大的免赔额。
  如果您的工作是探索海洋为生怎么办？希拉里·克鲁姆霍兹（Hillary Krumbholz）是加利福尼亚大学的一名研究潜水员，这意味着她每年夏天都会乘船出海，度过一天的时间，从蒙特雷湾，卡梅尔和大苏尔海岸潜水，进入海藻海底森林。在潜水良好的日子里，在偏远地区没人能入内，克鲁姆霍兹说，她确切地记得自己为什么喜欢她的工作。她告诉我，她绝对是在做自己梦dream以求的工作。
 她还指出，但是大部分工作是清理工作-清理个人设备，清理采样设备，清理组设备。当我问到听到有关她的工作的消息可能会令人们感到惊讶时，她想了一秒钟，然后说她一直在冷。 “我们带来了五加仑的热水桶，在水面停留期间，我们只是将手浸入这种热水中以进行加热，” Krumbholz说。就像我了解到的其他梦想工作一样，这还不足以继续生存：在淡季期间，Krumbholz还担任代课老师和水族馆海豚缸清洁工;由于购买大学健康保险意味着她在淡季期间经常会失去医疗保险，因此克鲁姆布尔兹决定改为继续在州立证券交易所交易。
 当然，梦dream以求的工作会更安全一些。对于某些草药而言，成为高薪公司律师是梦想。但是许多所谓的梦想工作看起来很像美国的大多数工作：不稳定，脆弱且是零工的一部分。
 这些工作涉及大量艰苦的工作，这不足为奇。也不会从做自己喜欢的事情的满意度中解脱出来。但是，正如吉尔·莱波（Jill Lepore）最近在《纽约客》中指出的那样，在工作中寻找意义的广泛观念是一个相当新的观念。毫不奇怪，这是老板的发明— 1970年代，工作不应该被改变的想法在专家们告诉Lepore时说，当时“管理人员开始通知工人，他们应该期望发现工作的人生目的。”
  对于那些幸运的人，他们现在比现在大流行之前仍能工作更长的时间，因此不难发现这种信息的易感性—当工人对自己的日常生活状况知之甚少时，为什么不集中精力在似乎由他们控制的事情上？做自己“爱”的事情有时会使长时间从事这种工作的影响变得平淡，而薪水却很少。尽管如此，有关激情和目标的信息的目标受众已按班级进行了深度分层-它主要针对非营利性，技术，媒体和其他行业（如首席娱乐官）的白领工人。 
但是关于热爱你所做的事情的信息可能会变成一种情况：在低收入行业工作的人，例如护理工作，有色人种女性和移民工人为主的领域，经常被剥削，部分原因是他们应该热爱自己的工作。 “家庭”一词通常在初创企业文化中很少被使用，在家庭保健等领域被字面上的理解，在这些领域中，工作赋予了生命，并且令人难以置信。这可以是忽略不计的低工资和其他工作场所虐待的框架（职务，家庭）。
 这并不是说热爱自己做的事情很糟糕，我们都想在生活中找到有意义的地方，许多梦想中的工作涉及关爱他人和自然世界，但某些工作却浪漫化可以掩盖一个简单的事实，即他们是工作，并且像任何工作一样，需要大量的组织来减少工作量。在公园护林员中，一个常见的口头禅是：“您会在日出和日落时得到报酬。”含义是，如果您正在努力实现自己的梦想，那么您将附带条件。史密斯告诉我，他讨厌那句话。他说：“日出和日落是美丽的，但它们不会把食物放在桌子上。”
 我们应该将工作理解为工作，无论您有多喜欢，没有工作都是例外。史密斯说，曾经有一位高级护林员坚持说：“给人们提供体面的工作，使他们过上体面的生活有什么问题？”这个现实听起来可能会放慢脚步，尤其是对于一个正在思考自己长大后想要成为什么样的孩子的孩子，但换一种方式来看，它也可能会很迷人。如果梦job以求的工作与其他任何工作一样，是不是让所有工作都做得更好？
note_en:
When I was young, my dream job was to be a horse. I didn’t want to be a horse girl, but a girl who is a horse. This made sense to me back then and also now. People respect horses, whose reputation is unimpeachable. Their work often consists of clear, concise tasks, like pulling a plow from one end of a field to another or carrying a child in circles around a fenced-in ring. Their basic needs are also generally met: At the end of the day, they get fed hay and their little iron shoes get cleaned out and they sleep in a house with high ceilings.
 My dream did not materialize, but as I entered the workforce, my early sense of what a dream job actually was only deepened. I wanted to do something I cared about and have my life taken care of in the process, but I always had some understanding that even the most fulfilling, best-paid job in the world would also still be work. After all, no one sees a horse pulling a cart and thinks that they aren’t laboring. Horses aren’t doing laps with fidgety nine-year-olds in their off-hours. No one says to a horse, “If you love what you do, you never work a day in your life.”
 Some of this is only becoming more obvious, including to myself, in an era when stable, full-time employment is evaporating, and holding on to any job is its own kind of feat. The Economic Policy Institute  calculates that the failed political response to the pandemic has left nearly 16 percent of the workforce—26.8 million people—unemployed or employed but losing hours and pay. And having work isn’t necessarily a guarantee against hardship—even before the current recession,  one in nine workers received wages too low to get out of poverty, even if they worked full-time hours. But the idea of a dream job is still catnip. There’s a slew of self-help articles advertising tips on how to  Land Your Dream Job by the End of the Year or  9 Genuine Steps to Quit Your Job and Fulfill Your Dream. Indeed.com lists info  for 25 dream jobs, three-quarters of which have an average national salary of less than $50,000 per year.
  A Mattress Firm internship, in which “ Snoozeterns” are  “encouraged” to sleep during work, making it literally a dream job, received more than 2,000 applicants. (One Snoozetern told me that she didn’t actually end up sleeping on the job, but she found the internship program fulfilling nonetheless.) There are Reddit threads for commonly romanticized jobs—park ranger, zookeeper, pilot—where posters ask detailed  questions  about how to actually make a career in one of these fields (“I love animals, but also have no training or special training with larger animals than a cat and dog. Is there anything I can do to work [at] a zoo with larger animals?”).
 Some dream job listings go viral, like the one from San Francisco’s East Brother Light Station, which advertised an opening for a couple to live and work in a historic lighthouse-turned-inn for $130,000 (total) per year. With people imagining an idyllic life out on the ocean—crucially, the lighthouse has no Wi-Fi—the East Brother Light Station ended up fielding thousands of applications, only 60 of them coming from actually qualified couples, since the job requires a valid U.S. Coast Guard license. But when, almost a year later,  The San Francisco Chronicle  interviewed Tiffany Danse and Tyler Waterson, the lucky people who landed the position, the dream seemed much more like, well, a job. “We are the reception desk. We run the gift shop. We make the beds. We cook the meals. Tyler is your captain and local history guide,” Danse said. The couple told the  Chronicle that they worked 80-to-90-hour weeks.
  It’s not hard to see why these kinds of jobs are so enticing. The concept of the dream job still persists, likely because so many of us are working in what the late David Graeber called “bullshit jobs,” or are simply not employed at all. Finding your dream job is a seductive idea: the do-gooder, Protestant version of  the FIRE movement—rather than trying to escape work, why not try loving it instead?
 It’s a relatable impulse, but I imagine most dream jobs are more like running a lighthouse bed-and-breakfast for 40 paying guests than a paid vacation. Andrew Smith, who has worked as an interpretive ranger at Glacier National Park for four summers, told me that he loves his job, which mostly involves mediating between park visitors and the park itself. There are clear perks to being a park ranger—getting to know a natural site day in and day out is a kind of intimate experience that tourists don’t get to experience. Smith lives in park housing and is happy that he spends his days mainly outdoors, the dream of many metro-locked middle-managers, even if much of it involves dealing with frustrated tourists who can’t find parking at a popular trailhead.
 But Smith told me that he still sees his job very much as work, with all the precarity that often comes with it. “Doing what you love as work is not the same as doing it as a hobby,” Smith said. “Going on a hike in uniform is just a fundamentally different experience than going out on my day off and enjoying the park that way.” Because Smith, like most national park employees, is a seasonal worker (right now he’s not employed by the park), his employment and benefits, while good during the park season, are unstable. Smith told me that some people leave park service when they turn 26 and can no longer stay on their parents’ health insurance. In the off-season, Smith works as a substitute teacher. Unless he lands a rare permanent park job, he can’t see it as a long-term career.
  Caroline Lange, a cookbook recipe tester, feels similarly lucky that she loves what she does. “My friend says it’s like a rom-com job, which I think is really accurate,” Lange said. “You don’t even realize it exists until you meet someone who does it.” But testing recipes isn’t quite like living in a Nancy Meyers film—Lange said that the majority of her job isn’t actually cooking, but schlepping groceries. It’s more physically demanding than people realize (she’s on her feet for much of the day), and since the gigs are freelance, Lange has no paid sick days and is insured on the most basic state exchange plan, which comes with an enormous deductible.
  What if your job was to explore the ocean for a living? Hillary Krumbholz is a research diver for a university in California, which means that every summer, she gets to go out on a boat and spend her days diving off the coasts of Monterey Bay, Carmel, and Big Sur, and into underwater kelp forests. The days when the dive is good, at remote sites no one can access, Krumbholz says that she remembers exactly why she loves her work; she tells me that she is definitely doing her dream job.
 But much of that job, she also notes, is cleaning up—cleaning up personal gear, cleaning up the sampling gear, cleaning up the group gear. When I asked what might surprise people to hear about her work, she thought for a second and then said that she’s constantly cold. “We bring five-gallon hot water buckets with us, and during our surface intervals, we’re just dipping our hands inside of this hot water trying to warm up,” Krumbholz said. And like other dream jobs I learned about, it’s not enough to survive on: During off-seasons,  Krumbholz  also works as a substitute teacher and an aquarium dolphin tank cleaner; since being on university health insurance would mean she’d constantly lose it during the off-season, Krumbholz decided to stay on the state exchange instead.
 There are certainly dream jobs that are more secure—the concept is a slippery one; for some herbs, being a high-paid corporate lawyer is the dream. But many so-called dream jobs look a lot like most jobs in America: unstable, tenuous, and part of a patchwork of gigs.
 That these jobs involve a good amount of grunt work is no big surprise; nor does it take away from the satisfaction of doing something you love. But the widespread notion of finding meaning in work is a fairly new one, as Jill Lepore  recently pointed out in  The New Yorker . Unsurprisingly, it’s an invention of bosses—the idea that work isn’t supposed to suck changed in the 1970s, experts told Lepore, when “managers began informing workers that they should expect to discover life’s purpose in work.”
  With people who are lucky enough to still have a job now  working longer hours than before the pandemic, it’s not hard to see the susceptibility to this kind of messaging—when workers have so little agency about the conditions of their daily life, why not focus on the things that seem to be in their control? Doing what you “love” can sometimes blunt the impact of doing that work for long hours and for little pay. Still, the intended audience for this messaging about passion and purpose is deeply stratified by class—it is largely targeted to white-collar workers in nonprofit, tech, media, and other industries with job titles like Chief Fun Officer.
 But the message around loving what you do can be warped to circumstance: People who work in low-income industries like care work, fields dominated by women of color and  immigrant workers, are often exploited in part because they are supposed to love what they do. The language of “family,” often weakly invoked in startup culture, is literalized in fields like home health care, where the work is life-giving and incredibly intimate. This can be the frame—duty, family—through which poor wages and other workplace abuses are ignored.
 This isn’t to say that loving what you do is bad—we all want to find meaning where we can in our lives, and many of these dream jobs involve caring for others and the natural world—but the romanticization of certain types of work can obscure the simple fact that they are jobs and, like any job, require mass organizing to suck less.  A common mantra among park rangers is, “You get paid in sunrises and sunsets.” The implication is that if you’re working your dream, you’ll take any conditions that come with it. Smith told me that he hates that saying. “The sunrises and sunsets are beautiful, but they don’t put food on the table,” he said.
 We should understand work as work, and that no job, no matter how much you love it, is an exception. Smith said that something a more senior ranger once said sticks with him: “What’s the issue with giving people decent jobs that allow them to have decent lives?” This reality might sound deflating, especially for a kid thinking about what they want to be when they grow up, but looked at another way, it can also be quite enchanting. If a dream job is like any other job, then isn’t making all jobs better the dream?
title: 梦Job以求的工作

note:
（路透社）-社交媒体网站Parler是受美国极右翼人士欢迎的应用程序，在一家俄罗斯独资技术公司的帮助下，部分在线返回。
  亚马逊（Parler）的用户呼吁施加暴力并发布视频以美化1月6日针对美国国会大厦的袭击事件后，帕勒（Parler）被亚马逊公司的托管部门和其他合作伙伴放慢脚步，从互联网上消失了。
 周一，Parler的网站再次可以访问，尽管只有其首席执行官的消息称他正在努力恢复功能。
 基础设施专家罗纳德·吉尔梅特（Ronald Guilmette）告诉路透社，它使用的互联网协议地址由DDos-Guard拥有，DDos-Guard由两名俄罗斯人控制，并提供包括分布式拒绝服务攻击防护在内的服务。
 如果网站完全恢复，Parler用户将可以查看和发表评论。但是，大多数用户都喜欢该应用程序，但苹果公司和谷歌官方商店仍禁止使用该应用程序。
  Parler首席执行官John Matze和DDoS-Guard的代表未回复置评请求。
 Matze上周三告诉路透社，该公司正在与多家服务提供商进行谈判，但拒绝透露细节。 
DDoS-Guard与其他种族主义，右派和阴谋网站合作，这些网站被大规模杀人犯用来分享信息，包括8kun。它还为俄罗斯政府站点提供了支持。
 吉尔梅特说，DDoS-Guard的网站以公司名称Cognitive Cloud LP列出了在苏格兰的一个地址，但该地址由俄罗斯顿河畔罗斯托夫的两个人拥有。其中一位告诉《卫报》，他并不了解公司提供的所有内容。
  批评者认为，依靠一家俄罗斯公司存在潜在的安全风险，对于自称爱国者的网站来说，这是一个奇怪的选择。
 俄罗斯的宣传已经激起了美国的政治分歧，支持即将离任的美国总统唐纳德·特朗普，并放大了有关选举舞弊的虚假叙述，还抗议警察的野蛮行径。
 透露其拥有超过1200万用户的Parler上周一起诉了亚马逊，此前这家电子商务巨头和云服务提供商中断了服务，理由是对暴力电话的控制不力。
 在周一的更新中，Parler.com与Fox News的一次采访相关，Matze表示，他“有信心” Parler将于1月底返回。
note_en:
(Reuters) - Parler, a social media website and app popular with the American far right, has partially returned online with the help of a Russian-owned technology company.
  Parler vanished from the internet when dropped by Amazon Inc’s hosting arm and other partners for poor moderation after its users called for violence and posted videos glorifying the Jan. 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol.
 On Monday, Parler’s website was reachable again, though only with a message from its chief executive saying he was working to restore functionality.
 The internet protocol address it used is owned by DDos-Guard, which is controlled by two Russian men and provides services including protection from distributed denial of service attacks, infrastructure expert Ronald Guilmette told Reuters.
 If the website is fully restored, Parler users would be able to see and post comments. Most users prefer the app, however, which remains banned from the official Apple Inc and Google stores.
  Parler CEO John Matze and representatives of DDoS-Guard did not reply to requests for comment.
 Last Wednesday, Matze told Reuters the company was in talks with multiple service providers but declined to elaborate.
 DDoS-Guard has worked with other racist, rightist and conspiracy sites that have been used by mass murderers to share messages, including 8kun. It has also supported Russian government sites.
 DDoS-Guard’s website lists an address in Scotland under the company name Cognitive Cloud LP, but that is owned by two men in Rostov-on-Don, Russia, Guilmette said. One of them told the Guardian recently that he was not aware of all of the content the company facilitates.
  Parler critics said it was a potential security risk for it to depend on a Russian company, as well as an odd choice for a site popular with self-described patriots.
 Russian propaganda has stoked political divisions in the United States, supporting outgoing U.S. President Donald Trump and amplifying false narratives about election fraud but also protests against police brutality.
 Parler, which disclosed it has over 12 million users, sued Amazon last Monday after the ecommerce giant and cloud services provider cut off service, citing poor moderation of calls to violence.
 In an update on Monday, Parler.com linked to a Fox News interview in which Matze said he was “confident” Parler would return at the end of January.
title: 在俄罗斯技术公司的支持下，Parler再次出现
title_en: Parler partially reappears with support from Russian technology firm

note:
2020年是科幻小说的一年，但这并不是要逃到其他世界。可以想象，幻想太空飞船会成为现实的重生之年，​​但是科幻小说和奇幻文学是人们对现实世界充满真正担忧的产物，因此，他们写下了我们在世界上看到的挑战在我们周围。在过去的12个月中，我一直在思考投机文学的价值。有一个模因可知，阅读是一种集体幻觉，是我们盯着枯树的碎片而得到的。那当然是正确的，但我想将科幻小说视为一种作弊指南或粗略的方向图。
 今年的书籍收录了我们周围发生的所有事情的脉动。但是，它们并不是吸引读者了解世界恶魔的方式-它们是富有思想的，有趣的故事，其角色扎根于您，与巨大的挑战作斗争。他们正在与压迫，财富不平等和种族主义作斗争。角色们都试图生存，建立新的世界，或拯救他们的朋友和家人免受伤害。总而言之，这些故事向我们展示了走出惨淡世界的道路，并一次进入了一页更好的一页。
    罗伯特·杰克逊·本内特（Robert Jackson Bennett）的《铸造厂》（Foundryside）是一部史诗般的奇幻小说中的数码朋克幻想。它的世界充满了魔力，通过一种名为“拼合”的过程为物体赋予了某种低水平的智力。在干预宇宙的源代码时，可能仅在某些情况下才试图打开一扇门，或者可能“确信”重力较高，从而促使其飞得更快。在那本书中，一位名叫桑西亚·格拉多（Sancia Grado）的女人被改成看到世界上潜在的魔法，并且与一些不太可能的盟友一起，拯救了特瓦讷（Tevanne）市免受破坏。
 在这部续集中，贝内特（Bennett）随着新的威胁出现，回到特凡妮（Tevanne）和桑西亚（Sancia）。在远古时代，一个名叫Crasedes的人通过努力的力量将自己变成了神，并最终被击败。千年之后，有人想出了使他复活的方法，他计划重塑世界和人类，以改善人类。当桑恰亚（Sancia）和她的盟友遇到克雷泽德斯（Crasedes）的超凡脱俗的恐怖，并努力反击他的计划时，这个最新的故事令人a目结舌。这部小说的核心是他的计划，贝内特（Bennett）对社会不平等如何破坏世界并导致革命性变化掌握得很好。
     杰耶·克拉克（P.DjélíClark）的短篇小说《响喊》的封面应该让您立即意识到眼前的威胁。 Ku Klux Klan于1922年成立于佐治亚州，得到了复兴的支持，但并没有得到您所期望的地方：他们的行列被吸引到仇恨和种族主义，并计划使用魔法的超凡脱俗的生物渗透（以种族主义电影《国家的诞生》的形式）。面对他们的是三个黑人妇女，玛丽丝（Maryse），萨迪（Sadie）和科迪莉亚（Cordelia），他们从一次世界大战中走出来，具有致命的技能，并愿意用它来与我们世界和他人的邪恶作斗争。
 在抗议警察暴行和种族不平等的夏天之后，克拉克的书达到了宣泄正义与稀缺冒险小说之间的平衡，因为玛丽斯和她的同伴开始了解他们面临的洛夫克拉夫特式威胁。举世无双的Ku Kluxes通过种族主义者和偏执狂找到了进入我们世界的简便途径，但是Maryse知道力量在腐化，即使她的事业是公正的，她也可以轻易地通过毁灭性的道路摧毁自己的家园和一切战斗对于。
    一个名叫皮拉内西（Piranesi）的人在看似无尽的迷宫中独居，每个房间都有自己的风格和特色。它的走廊两旁排列着各种类型的雕像，在那堵墙内是一个被困的海洋，起伏不定。皮拉涅西（Piranesi）的任务是尽可能多地探索自己的世界，而其他人类的唯一证据就是13具骨骼和一个名为“他者”的人。 
皮拉内西和另一方正在寻求对自己世界的更好的理解，当另一方向皮拉内西询问第16个人时，他开始怀疑他们共同的世界和他自己的过去比他意识到的要多得多。从本质上讲，克拉克创作了一部精美的小说，讲述了我们周围的墙壁和世界，以及质疑世界本质的重要性。
    Alix E. Harrow执导了梦幻般的世界首演《一月的一千个门》之后，讲述了三姐妹Agnes Amaranth，Beatrice Belladonna和James Juniper的故事，他们在塞勒姆经过多年疏远后重新团结起来，成为了妇女的风口浪尖。选举权运动。塞勒姆当然是1600年代后期对女巫的审判的代名词，而巫术在全国各地已被广泛淘汰。三姐妹意识到，妇女不仅寻求投票权，而且还威胁着以男性为主导的世界的巫术的基本力量。
 这本书很高兴在2020年阅读，特别是在总统大选争议之后。哈罗对这部小说充满了对女性权利历史的深刻理解，而这本书激怒了男人如何找到使妇女脱离平等几个世纪的方法。巫术和魔法可能是情节的焦点，但放逐和定罪只是当权者在整个历史上发现破坏妇女的一种方式。
    N.K. Jemisin无疑是当今从事推测性小说创作的最好的作家之一：她的《破碎的地球》三部曲赢得了她无数的赞誉，而今年早些时候，John D.和Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation任命她为MacArthur研究员。
 她经常在自己的书中探讨种族主义的性质及其影响，在她的最新作品《我们成为城市》中，她对纽约市的绅士化进行了梦幻般的旋转，通过举世无双的Lovecraftian宇宙恐怖的镜头探索了种族主义。从她的短篇小说《伟大的城市诞生》开始，我们了解到世界上每个主要出生城市的化身都是人类的化身-它们是人类形态的精髓，纽约市为每个行政区创建了多个。
 当这些化身醒来并开始实现其目标时，他们也开始意识到自己面临着一个新的威胁-白人妇女-想要毁灭我们的世界并为自己的超世俗目的而改变它。杰米森（Jemisin）向城市和使城市变得更美好的人们编织了一封精彩的情书，以及白人至上主义和高级化等力量如何齐头并进，以破坏其活力和精神。
    在人类在火星上建立殖民地之后，科幻小说将成为一个长期的悲剧：他们开始抵抗地球的统治之前需要多长时间？这两个世界在文化上将如何彼此分离？ 
这就是郝敬芳的首本小说《流浪汉》的前提。一个世纪前，火星与地球争夺独立，并获胜。如今，余烬已经降温，火星已开始派出首批跨文化使者（称为水星集团）到地球，以重建外交和文化纽带。
 五年后返回家园，水星小组的成员发现自己陷入了两个截然不同的世界：超级资本主义地球和更集体/社会主义的火星。他们对在家中的生活和机会感到幻灭，一名成员罗英（火星领袖的孙女）开始质疑她的家人在世界分隔中的作用。郝的故事是缓慢的故事，但我们对分开我们的文化差异和不平等现象进行了很好的考察。
    如果曾经有一段时间，感觉就像我们无意中跳入了错误的时间表，那么今年就到了。多重替代世界是米卡亚·约翰逊（Micaiah Johnson）在其首本小说《世界之间的空间》中饰演的一种斜体，该小说跟随一个名叫卡拉（Cara）的年轻女子在世界之间旅行。几年前，一个名叫亚当·博世（Adam Bosch）的人发现了一种前往其他现实世界的方法-大约有400个。旅行者可以旅行，但只能在他们自己没有自己的情况下进行。进入卡拉，她在世界末日后的贫民窟中长大，她的另类自我似乎很不幸：她可以游历372个不同的世界。
 在Eldridge研究所工作以收集有关各个世界的数据，但是即使她以旅行者身份获得新的特权，她仍然在两个世界之间陷入困境：由于其他自我的不幸，她对公司仅具有宝贵的地位-这种状态很容易瞬间消失。约翰逊透过卡拉（Cara），着眼于经济和种族不平等与特权的大主题，并通过力图改变世界的强大人物的眼光，看待我们设定的界限如何定义我们。
    在斯蒂芬·格雷厄姆·琼斯（Stephen Graham Jones）的最新小说《唯一的印第安人》（The Only Good Indians）的开场时刻中，一名黑脚男子在面对一个停车场的麋鹿后，在中西部一家酒吧的停车场被杀。里奇（Ricky）是他的四个朋友之一，他们很快就会遇到麋鹿，因为他们十年前做出的选择获得了回报。
 四重奏组是在保留地上长大的，前往禁猎区，试图在狩猎季节结束前杀死对方。他们中奖了：一群麋鹿躲在暴风雪中，他们很快跌落下来。十年后，其中一个受害者的精神又回来了，并开始迅速追踪他们以进行报复。
 琼斯构建了一个令人着迷的恐怖故事，使您在完成每个男人的生活之前进入他们的生活。但是，尽管在最后一幕中肯定有恐怖，但他巧妙地通过给每个人都带来偏执，恐惧和贪婪，加剧了紧张局势，并利用了针对土著美国人的种族主义不平等现象。 
几年前，玛丽·罗宾妮特·科瓦尔（Mary Robinette Kowal）撰写了短片《火星女宇航员》，这是继“女宇航员”艾尔玛·约克（Elma York）在一场毁灭性的小行星撞击后揭开序幕之后的短篇小说。
 在她的最新作品《无情的月亮》中，科瓦尔在有经验的宇航员妮可·沃金（Nicole Wargin）负责在月球基地领导安全工作之后，进一步拉开了其中一些主题的篇章。由于宗教极端分子致力于破坏火箭和该计划的设施，该太空计划正遭受动荡的威胁，而沃金（Wargin）竭力保护其工作的人们安全。
 在整个系列中，科瓦尔都研究了不平等现象，这些不平等现象在我们自己的真实太空计划中将有色人种和宇航员拒之门外。无情的月亮很好地观察了人们在特殊情况下如何承受极端压力下的创伤，精神疾病和不平等现象。
    墨西哥名媛NoemíTaboada的父亲收到表妹的一封令人痛苦的信后，将她派遣到墨西哥乡村的一个名为High Place的乡村庄园，信中写道，她被违背了她的意愿，房子里满是鬼。 Noemí所发现的不只是一个生病的亲戚：一个腐朽的英国家庭的阴险阴谋带有令人恐惧的秘密。
 西尔维娅·莫雷诺·加西亚（Silvia Moreno-Garcia）令人振奋的奇妙小说包含了一个哥特式故事中您所期望的所有装置：一个家庭，过去已经辉煌一整年，拥有奇异的超自然影响力，还有一座破败的美好家庭。当诺米（Noemí）努力找出在高地（High Place）发生的奇怪事件时，莫雷诺·加西亚（Moreno-Garcia）勾勒出一个坚定而有能力的女主人公，他们会为保护自己和家人而竭尽全力。
 莫雷诺·加西亚（Moreno-Garcia）通过利用欧洲殖民和征服墨西哥的历史，超越了单纯的敬意和恐惧。高地的历史及其血腥的压迫性遗产是Noemí发现的场景不可或缺的一部分，并且其布局被巧妙地布置，使人读起来既吸引人又深思熟虑。
    Tochi Onyebuchi的首本成人小说是在现代背景下创作的，紧随其后的是一位名叫Ella的年轻黑人妇女和她的兄弟Kev，后者在1990年代初的洛杉矶罗德尼·金暴动期间出生。埃拉（Ella）具有一些特殊的力量：她可以看到未来，飞翔并将自己投射到其他地方。随着故事的进展，两个孩子长大了，凯夫（Kev）最终被警察和司法系统逮捕，残酷和入狱。 
监禁多年以来，他的姐姐拜访了他，姐姐忍受了残酷的刑罚，使他保持理智。埃拉（Ella）竭尽所能，在离开自己的牢房并进入一个崭新的高科技和反乌托邦世界后，无奈地改变自己的处境。 Onyebuchi的那本纤细的书读得很厉害，它激怒了针对黑人的压迫和暴力的周期性性质，以及他们如何摆脱困境。
    丽贝卡·罗恩霍斯（Rebecca Roanhorse）的最新著作与她的前两部小说《闪电的踪迹》和《蝗虫风暴》有些不同。在这两个城市的幻想是轻风轻拂，激动人心的惊悚片，以及在未来受到气候变化破坏的梦幻般的美国的情况下，《黑太阳》是关于美国本土化世界中权力斗争的雄心勃勃的幻想。
 随着冬至临近，托瓦市已准备好举行盛大的庆祝活动，而没有意识到一个被遗忘的氏族所产生的威胁，该氏族曾经被该市的一名太阳神父屠杀其信仰。腐肉乌鸦氏族的其余成员没有忘记灭绝他们的企图，并派出了一种特殊的武器-一个复仇的男孩来复仇。罗阿诺斯（Roanhorse）的小说令人心碎（黑暗），看待创伤对几代人的影响，以及角色如何与他们所处的系统抗争。
    如果说今年有什么书令我深受打击，那就是金·斯坦利·罗宾逊（Kim Stanley Robinson）的《未来部》，这是一部关于气候变化和人类为时已晚的努力扭转困境的努力的史诗。
 罗宾逊以前曾探讨过这些主题：他的小说《极光》是对太空探索可能的现实写照，并最终得出结论，我们真正需要在进入宇宙之前专注于地球；纽约2140跟随一个溺水城市中的一系列人物，观察灾难袭击后气候变化的影响。
 未来事务部向前迈进了几十年，而罗宾逊则对我们可能的前景提出了令人恐惧的印象。地球面临灾难性的热浪，在数周之内杀死了数千万的热浪，大规模的迁徙和冲突都是由一个快速变暖的星球驱动的。通过旨在拯救地球的机构成员以及各章探讨从无畏科学家到阻止南极冰川掉入海洋到氢和碳原子的一切事物，罗宾逊列出了我们可能采取的艰难步骤以改变我们的生活方式来拯救植物。
     回顾过去十年左右的有关人工智能和机器学习的头条新闻，并以此为基础讲一个有关实验性警察机器人与FBI特工合作的故事。那是P.W.的前提辛格和奥古斯特·科尔（August Cole）的最新技术是从近乎未来的技术破灭者中获得的，这本书构成了一篇引人入胜的读物，讲述了我们不久的将来的机器人技术可能看起来像什么。 
联邦调查局特工拉拉·基根（Lara Keegan）的任务是评估TAMS（战术自主移动系统）作为该局的潜在新工具。当华盛顿特区面临新的威胁时，它就派上用场了：一名技术不利的极端主义者，正准备在各个薄弱环节进攻这座城市-对基础设施发动网络攻击，并使用无人机对人民进行攻击。 Singer和Cole来自政策和智囊团，他们不仅关注我们当前的技术生活所带来的潜在威胁，而且还研究日益壮大的白人民族主义运动似乎准备利用这些问题。
    1714年，一个名叫阿德琳（Adeline）的年轻女子在她家乡法国萨尔特河畔维隆（Villon-sur-Sarthe）的家中安静地生活。她渴望获得更多的东西–远赴自己熟悉的世界旅行，并且做的不仅仅是嫁给同一个村民。当她遇到一个超凡脱俗的陌生人，她答应答应自己的愿望时，她就准备好结婚。
 熟悉浮士德的人都知道，这样的交易成本很高。艾德琳（Adeline）可以活到她想要的任何时候，完全可以自由探索世界，但是没有人会记住她。在接下来的三个世纪中，除了她隐蔽的恩人之外，她完全独自一人在世界上奔波，从一个地方移到另一个地方，学习如何生存。当男孩第一次记住她时，所有情况在2014年发生了变化。 V.E. Schwab的故事令人感动，令人心碎，并强调了我们在生活中建立联系的重要性，即使在充满虚拟会议，远距离聚会和独自在家中度过的一年中。
    玛莎·威尔斯（Martha Wells）的四本Murderbot中篇小说树立了一个引人注目的角色：一个安全机器人，摆脱了内部主管的束缚，并自称为Murderbot。但是，与《终结者》不同，它只想独自一人观看肥皂剧，避免讨厌的人。 《网络效应》是威尔斯在该系列中的第一部长篇小说（这不是最后的冒险-另一部中篇小说《逃亡的遥测》（Fugitive Telemetry），将于明年出版），并且像它的前辈一样，它是对人类和意识的探索。
 Network Effect发现Murderbot及其同伴被不知名的袭击者伏击并俘获，迫使android采取严厉行动以保持其不情愿的人的安全。威尔斯迫使Murderbot面对它不愿做的事情，并迫使它意识到关怀人不是一件坏事，即使亲人和朋友（即使他们可能会很烦恼），您也可以将自己带到身边这个圈子是有价值的，不仅是因为他们可能会立即提供帮助，而且是为了一个人在世界上的存在感。在一年中，我们不得不与许多人保持距离，这是一个关于我们与周围的人形成的联系以及这些联系如何使世界更美好的有力故事。
    在遥远的未来，人类已经发展到可以扩散到星空的水平，使我们能够在遥远的世界定居下来。人们会陷入停滞状态，或者上传自己的意识以成为高级人工智能，从而在长距离和长时间的空间中生存。在尤丹贾亚·维耶拉特尼（Yudhanjaya Wijeratne）的最新小说《救助船员》中，一名由佛教徒转变为AI的诗人都希望他跋涉到Urmahon Beta成为拆下一艘被击落的联合国殖民船的例行公事。
 他和他的助手们西蒙，安娜和米洛的杂乱无章的工作人员发现，在地球表面上存在着更具挑战性的环境，充满了大型动物，控制论的信徒和疾病，它们在试图从垃圾中收集足够的垃圾时威胁着他们的生存。船舶残骸获利。维耶拉特涅（Wijeratne）展开了一场神话般的太空冒险，随着他的角色被迫面对人类的意义以及地球以外的智慧，他意外地将其转变为哲学。

note_en:
2020 was quite the year for science fiction, but it wasn’t all about escaping to other worlds. It’s easy to imagine flights of fancy in a spaceship to be a reprieve to reality, but science fiction and fantasy literature is the product of people with real concerns about the real world, and accordingly,  they write about the challenges that we see in the world around us. Over the last 12 months, I’ve been thinking about the value of speculative literature in a time like this. There’s a meme going around that reading is a collective hallucination that we get by staring at bits of a dead tree. That’s certainly accurate, but I like to think of science fiction as a sort of cheat guide or rough map of directions.
 This year’s crop of books are ones that have a thumb on the pulse of everything that’s been going on around us. But they’re not screeds lecturing readers about the evils of the world — they’re thoughtful, interesting stories with characters that you root for, fighting against huge challenges. They’re fighting against oppression, wealth inequality, and racism. The characters are all trying to survive, to build new worlds, or save their friends and family from harm. Collectively, they’re the stories that show us the way out of a dismal world and into a slightly better one, one page at a time.
    Robert Jackson Bennett’s  Foundryside was a cyberpunk fantasy wrapped up in an epic fantasy novel. It’s set in a world where magic permeates everything, imbuing objects with a sort of low-level intelligence through a process called scriving. In meddling with the source code of the universe, a door might be scrived to open only under a certain set of circumstances, or an arrow might be “convinced” that gravity is higher, prompted it to fly faster than it might otherwise. In that book, a woman named Sancia Grado has been altered to see the underlying magic in the world, and with some unlikely allies, saved the city of Tevanne from destruction.
 In this sequel, Bennett returns to Tevanne and Sancia as a new threat emerges. In ancient times, a man named Crasedes turned himself into a god through the power of scriving, and was eventually defeated. After millennia, someone has figured out how to resurrect him, and he plans to remake the world and humanity, in the effort to improve humanity. This latest tale is a gripping read as Sancia and her allies encounter the otherworldly horror that Crasedes is, and work to counter his plans. At the heart of the novel is the mechanics of his plans, and Bennett has a good grip on how inequalities in society break the world and lead to revolutionary change.
     The cover of P. Djèlí Clark’s short novel  Ring Shout should gives you an immediate sense of the threat at hand. Set in 1922 in Georgia, the Ku Klux Klan experiences a resurgence in support, but not from the places you’d expect: their ranks are being infiltrated by otherworldly creatures who are drawn to their hatred and racism, and who are planning to use magic (in the form of the racist movie  Birth of a Nation). Facing them are three black women, Maryse, Sadie, and Cordelia, who have come out of the First World War with a deadly skillset and a willingness to use it to fight against the evils of our world and others.
 After a summer of protests against police brutality and racial inequality, Clark’s book strikes a balance of cathartic justice and pulpy adventure fiction as Maryse and her companions begin to understand the Lovecraftian threat they face. The otherworldly Ku Kluxes have found an easy avenue into our world through racists and bigots, but Maryse learns that power corrupts, and that even if her cause is just, she can easily turn down a devastating path that will destroy her home and everything she’s fought for.
    A man named Piranesi lives a solitary existence within a seemingly endless labyrinth of rooms, each with their own flavor and character. Its hallways are lined with statues of all types, and within those walls is a trapped ocean, which ebbs and flows. Piranesi has made it his mission to explore as much of his world as possible, and the only other evidence of other humans are 13 skeletons and a man called the Other.
 Piranesi and the Other are searching for a better understanding of what their world is, and when the Other asks Piranesi about a 16th person, he begins to suspect that there is far more to their shared world and his own past than he realized. At its heart, Clarke has produced an exquisite novel about the walls and world that surrounds us, and the importance of questioning the very nature of the world.
    After her fantastic, world-hopping debut  The Ten Thousand Doors of January, Alix E. Harrow follows the story of three sisters, Agnes Amaranth, Beatrice Belladonna, and James Juniper, who reunite after years of estrangement in Salem on the cusp of the Women’s Suffrage Movement. Salem, of course, is synonymous with its witch trials of the late 1600s, and witchcraft has been largely stamped out throughout the country. The three sisters realize that women not only seek the right to vote, but also the foundational power of witchcraft that threatens the male-dominated world.
 The book was a delight to read in 2020, especially in the aftermath of a contentious presidential election. Harrow imbues the novel with fiery understanding of the history of women’s rights, and the book is brimming with anger over how men have found ways to shut women out from equality for centuries. Witchcraft and magic might be the focus of the plot, but its banishment and criminalization is just one way that those in power have found to undermine women throughout history.
    N.K. Jemisin is easily one of the best living writers working with speculative fiction today: her Broken Earth trilogy earned her numerous accolades, and earlier this year, the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation named her a MacArthur Fellow for her body of work.
 She frequently explores the nature of racism and its fallout in her books, and in her latest,  The City We Became, she puts a fantastical spin on the gentrification of New York City, exploring racism through the lens of upended, Lovecraftian cosmic horror. Jumping off from her short story “ The City Born Great”, we learn that the world’s major cities each birth avatars — their essence in human form, and New York City has created several, one for each borough.
 As these avatars awaken and begin to realize their purpose, they also begin to realize that they face a new threat — a woman in white – who looks to destroy our world and remake it for her own, otherworldly purposes. Jemisin weaves together a wonderful love letter to the city and the people who make it great, and how forces like white supremacy and gentrification go hand-in-hand to destroy its vibrancy and spirit.
    A long-standing trope in science fiction is what would happen after humans establish a colony on Mars: how long would it be before they begin to resist governance from Earth, and how would the two worlds diverge from one another, culturally?
 That’s the premise behind Hao Jingfang’s debut novel,  Vagabonds. A century ago, Mars fought Earth in a bid for independence, and won. Now that the embers have cooled, Mars has begun to send its first cross-cultural emissaries (known as the Mercury Group) to Earth to reestablish diplomatic and cultural ties.
 After returning home after five years, the members of the Mercury Group find themselves caught between two very different worlds: hyper-capitalist Earth and a more collective / socialistic Mars. They’re disillusioned with their lives and opportunities back at home, and one member, Luoying — the granddaughter of a Martian leader — begins to question her family’s role in the separation between the worlds. Hao’s story is a slow burn, but excellent examination at the cultural differences and inequalities that separate us.
    If ever there was a time that it felt like we accidentally skipped onto the wrong timeline, this year was it. Multiple, alternate worlds is a trope that Micaiah Johnson plays with in her debut novel,  The Space Between Worlds, which follows a young woman named Cara as she travels between worlds. Years earlier, a man named Adam Bosch discovered a method for traveling to other, alternate realities — around 400 of them. A traveller could make the trip, but only in realities in which their alternate selves weren’t around. Enter Cara, who grew up in the post-apocalyptic slums and who’s alternative selves seem to have bad luck: she can travel to 372 different worlds.
 Working for the Eldridge Institute to collect data about the various worlds, but even with her new, privileged status as a Traveller, she’s caught between worlds: she’s only valuable to the company because of the misfortunes of her alternate selves — a status that could easily vanish in a snap. Through Cara, Johnson looks at big picture themes of economic and racial inequality and privilege, and how the boundaries we set up define who we are, all through the eyes of a powerful character looking to change the world.
    In the opening moments of Stephen Graham Jones’ latest novel,  The Only Good Indians, a Blackfoot man is killed in the parking lot of a midwestern bar after confronting an elk in a parking lot. Ricky is one of his four friends who will soon encounter the elk, spectral payback for choices they made a decade ago.
 The quartet grew up on a reservation and headed out to a forbidden area to try and bag a kill before the end of hunting season. They hit the jackpot: a herd of elk sheltering in the midst of a snow storm, and who quickly fall to their bullets. A decade later, a spirit of one of their victims has returned and begins to quickly track them down to exact its revenge.
 Jones has constructed a gripping horror tale, one that brings you into the lives of each man before finishing them off. But while there’s certainly horror in the final acts, he masterfully builds up the tension by injecting each with paranoia, fear, and greed, tapping into the racism inequality that’s directed towards indigenous Americans.
    A couple of years ago, Mary Robinette Kowal wrote “The Lady Astronaut of Mars,” a short story following “lady astronaut” Elma York in an alternate space race that kicked off after a devastating asteroid strike on Earth.
 In her latest,  The Relentless Moon, Kowal tugs further at some of those themes, following experienced astronaut Nicole Wargin as she’s tasked with heading up security on a lunar base. The space program has come under threat as unrest as religious extremists work to sabotage rockets and the program’s facilities, while Wargin fights to keep the people she works with safe.
 Throughout the series, Kowal has looked at the inequalities that shut out women and astronauts of color in our own real space program.  The Relentless Moon takes an excellent look at how people cope with trauma, mental illness, and inequalities under extreme pressure in extraordinary situations.
    Mexican socialite Noemí Taboada is dispatched to a rural estate called High Place in rural Mexican after her father receives a distressing letter from her cousin, writing that she’s being held against her will and that the house is full of ghosts. What Noemí discovers is more than an ill relative: a sinister plot on the part of a decaying English family with a horrifying secret.
 Silvia Moreno-Garcia’s electrifying and wonderful novel contains all the fixtures that you’d expect from a gothic story: a family that’s long since past its glory days, strange supernatural influences, and a wonderful, crumbling family home. As Noemí works to figure out the strange happenings at High Place, Moreno-Garcia sketches out a determined and empowered heroine who’ll stop at nothing to save herself and her family.
 Moreno-Garcia goes beyond mere genre homage and bulds on the horror by utilizing the history of the European colonization and conquest of Mexico. The history of High Place and its bloody, oppressive legacy is integral to the scene that Noemí discovers, and its reveal is masterfully laid out, making for a gripping and thoughtful read.
    Tochi Onyebuchi’s debut adult novel is set in the modern day, following a young Black woman named Ella and her brother Kev, who was born in the midst of the Los Angeles Rodney King riots in the early 1990s. Ella has some special powers: she can see the future, fly, and project herself to other places. As the story progresses, the two children grow up, and Kev winds up arrested, brutalized, and jailed by the police and justice system.
 Over the years while he’s incarcerated, he’s visited by his sister, who helped to keep him sane as he endures a brutal sentence. Ella, with all of her powers, is helpless to change his circumstances after he leaves his cell and into a new, high-tech and dystopian world. Onyebuchi’s slim book is a powerful read that brims with anger at the cyclical nature of oppression and violence directed at Black people, and how they might break free.
    Rebecca Roanhorse’s latest book is a bit of a departure from her first two novels,  Trail of Lightning and  Storm of Locusts. Where those two urban fantasies were breezy, action-packed thrillers set in a future and fantastical United States ravaged by climate change,  Black Sun is an ambitious fantasy about a power struggle in an Indigenous American-inspired world.
 As a winter solstice approaches, the city of Tova readies itself for a great celebration, unaware of a threat that’s emerged from a forgotten clan that was once massacred for their beliefs by one of the city’s Sun Priests. The remaining members of the Carrion Crow clan haven’t forgotten the attempt to exterminate them, and have sent along a special weapon — a boy raised as a weapon revenge — to exact revenge for those crimes. Roanhorse’s novel is a heartbreaking (and dark) look at the impact that trauma has over generations, and how the characters involved fight against the systems that they’re caught within.
    If there’s any book that hit me hard this year, it was Kim Stanley Robinson’s  The Ministry for the Future, a sweeping epic about climate change and humanity’s efforts to try and turn the tide before it’s too late.
 Robinson has explored these themes before: his novel  Aurora is a realistic take on what space exploration might look like, and ultimately comes to the conclusion that we really need to focus on Earth before we go out into the cosmos;  New York 2140 follows a series of characters in a drowned city, looking at what the impact of climate change might after catastrophe strikes.
 The Ministry For The Future jumps just a couple of decades forward, and Robinson presents a horrifying look at what we might be in for. Earth faces cataclysmic heat waves that kill tens of millions in weeks, massive migrations, and conflicts all driven by a rapidly-warming planet. Through the members of an agency designed to save the planet — as well as with chapters exploring everything from intrepid scientists working to stop Antarctica’s glaciers from falling into the ocean to hydrogen and carbon atoms — Robinson lays out the likely hard steps that we’ll have to take to change our way of life to save the plant.
     Take the headlines about artificial intelligence and machine learning from the last decade or so, and use that as the basis to tell a story about an experimental police robot being partnered up with an FBI agent. That’s the premise of P.W. Singer and August Cole’s latest ripped-from-the-near-future technothriller, in which they construct a gripping read about what our near future of robotics might plausibly look like.
 FBI Special Agent Lara Keegan is tasked with evaluating TAMS (Tactical Autonomous Mobility System) as a potential new tool for the Bureau. It comes in handy as Washington DC faces a new threat: a technology-adverse extremist who is looking to attack the city at its various weak points — launching cyberattacks against infrastructure and using drones to conduct assaults against people. Singer and Cole come from the policy and think tank worlds, and look at not only the potential threats that our current technological lives bring, but how the growing white nationalist movement seems poised to take advantage of those problems.
    In 1714, a young woman named Adeline lives a quiet existence in her home village of Villon-sur-Sarthe in France. She yearns for something more — to travel beyond the world she’s familiar with, and to do more than just marry a fellow villager. She’s poised for such a marriage when she encounters an otherworldly stranger that promises to grant her wish, which she eagerly accepts.
 As anyone who’s familiar with Faust knows, such bargains come with a high cost. Adeline will live for as long as she wants, completely free to explore the world, but nobody will remember her. For the next three centuries, she gets her bearings on the world, moving from place to place and learning to survive, completely alone in the world, except for her shadowy benefactor. All of that changes in 2014 when a boy remembers her for the first time. V.E. Schwab’s story is emotional and heartbreaking, and highlights the importance of those connections we make as we move through life — even in a year filled with virtual meetings, distanced gatherings, and time spent alone in our homes.
    Martha Wells’s four Murderbot novellas set up a compelling character: a security robot that’s broken free of its internal governors, and which has taken to calling itself Murderbot. But unlike the Terminator, it just wants to be left alone to watch soap operas and avoid pesky humans.  Network Effect is Wells’ first full-length novel in the series (it’s not the last adventure — another novella,  Fugitive Telemetry, is due out next year), and like its predecessors, it’s an exploration of humanity and consciousness.
 Network Effect finds Murderbot and its companions are ambushed and captured by unknown assailants, forcing the android to take drastic action to keep the people it’s reluctantly come to care about safe. Wells forces Murderbot to confront the things that it’s reluctant to do, and forces it to realize that caring for people isn’t a bad thing, and that the people and friends — even if they can be annoying — you bring around you in your immediate circle are valuable, not just for the help that they might immediately provide, but for one’s sense of being in the world. In a year where we’ve had to put many people at a physical distance, it’s a powerful story about the connections we form with those around us, and how those connections make for a better world.
    In the distant future, humanity has advanced to the point where we’re able to spread into the stars, allowing us to settle down on distant worlds. People survive the long distances and times of space by going into stasis, or uploading their consciousnesses to become advanced artificial intelligences. In Yudhanjaya Wijeratne’s latest novel,  The Salvage Crew, OC, a Buddist-turned-AI poetry enthusiast expects his trek to Urmahon Beta to be a routine run to pick apart a downed UN colony ship.
 What he and his motley crew of helpers, Simon, Anna, and Milo, find on the planet’s surface is a far more challenging environment, full of megafauna, cybernetic cultists, and diseases that threaten their survival while they try and pull together enough junk from the ship’s wreckage to make a profit. Wijeratne spins together a fabulous space adventure that takes an unexpected turn into the philosophical as his characters are forced to confront what it means to be human, and what intelligence beyond Earth might look like.

......
title: 赶上2020年的科幻小说和奇幻小说
title_en: Sci-fi and fantasy books of 2020 to catch up with
